coredata.dc:title: Peroxyoxalate/carbon dots chemiluminescent reaction for fluorescent and visual determination of Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Jia
Deng, Zhiqin
Ding, Caihe
Jin, Yanzi
Wang, Bin
Chen, Jiucun
coredata.dc:description: A visible luminous sensing platform was studied by using peroxyoxalate-based chemiluminescent (PO CL) system and fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). The new CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of red pitaya, which owned abundant O-related surface functional groups and thus displayed high sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ ion. Based on peroxyoxalate/carbon dots chemiluminescent (PO/CDs CL) reaction, a visible luminous sensing platform was constructed, in which CDs acted as capturers and emitters for Fe3+ identification and luminescence signal. Using this technique, a trace of Fe3+ ion can be simply and efficiently distinguished without instrumental assistance. It turns out that the proposed method has good sensitivity, selectivity and potential application in sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Peroxyoxalate
Fe3+
Chemiluminescence
Optical sensor
H2O2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image and size distribution histogram (A), XPS spectra (B), High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (C) and O1s (D), FTIR spectra (E) and Raman spectra (F) of the as-prepared CDs.
(A) UV–vis absorption and FL emission spectra of the CDs (λex 350 nm) and photograph of the CDs under UV light (inset), (B) FL emission spectra of the CDs under excitation with different wavelengths.

The UV–vis absorption (A) and FL emission spectra (B) of the CDs (a), Fe3+ (b) and CDs + Fe3+ (c). Inset: the corresponding photography of the CDs and CDs + Fe3+ solution under exciting light at 365 nm. (C) Representative emission spectra of the CDs upon the addition of increasing Fe3+ concentrations in the 10 mM PBS buffer at pH 7.0. (D) The relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and Fe3+ concentration. Inset: linearity of fluorescence signal towards Fe3+ (the error bars obtained from the standard deviation of three measurements).
The selectivity of the detection towards common interfering ion in 10 mM PBS (pH = 7.0) (the concentration of these ions were 2 μM).

(A) Emission spectra of CDs under 365 nm irradiation (a), and without exciting light (b). Emission spectra of solution I + II (c), and the mixture of solution I + II + CDs (d) in the darkness. Inset: the corresponding photographs. (B) The chemiluminescent decay curves and corresponding photographs of the PO/CDs CL system. Inset: photographs of the system at different time. (All photographs were taken using smartphone camera).

(A) Emission spectra of the PO/CDs CL system with addition of Fe3+ (a-c: Fe3+ concentration was 0, 0.5, and 5.0 mM, respectively; Inset: the corresponding photographs in darkness). (B) Quantification of Fe3+ by the PO/CDs CL system. (C) The linear relationship between the luminous intensity and the Fe3+ concentration. (D) The corresponding photos of Fe3+ sensing under that light (above) and in darkness (below) (from left to right: Fe3+ concentration was 0, 0.03, 0.3, 3.0, 30, 300 and 3000 μM, respectively).
Schematic illustration of the chemiluminescent reaction mechanism of PO/CDs.

The selectivity of the detection of Fe3+ by the PO/CDs CL system. (A) The fluorescence response of the system toward metal ions. (B) Corresponding photos under the light (above) and darkness (below). The concentration of all metal ions was 3 mM.

head.ce:title: Peroxyoxalate/carbon dots chemiluminescent reaction for fluorescent and visual determination of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Engineering Research Center for Micro-Nano Biomedical Materials and Devices, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Engineering Research Center for Micro-Nano Biomedical Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Engineering Research Center for Micro-Nano Biomedical Materials and Devices, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Clean Energy and Advanced Materials, School of Materials and Energy, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Clean Energy and Advanced Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Clean Energy and Advanced Materials, School of Materials and Energy, School of Materials and Energy, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally: J. Huang, Z. Deng, C. Ding.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A visible luminous sensing platform based on peroxyoxalate/CDs chemiluminescent reaction was constructed, in which CDs acted as capturers and emitters for Fe3+ identification and luminescence signal.

A visible luminous sensing platform was studied by using peroxyoxalate-based chemiluminescent (PO CL) system and fluorescent carbon dots (CDs). The new CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of red pitaya, which owned abundant O-related surface functional groups and thus displayed high sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ ion. Based on peroxyoxalate/carbon dots chemiluminescent (PO/CDs CL) reaction, a visible luminous sensing platform was constructed, in which CDs acted as capturers and emitters for Fe3+ identification and luminescence signal. Using this technique, a trace of Fe3+ ion can be simply and efficiently distinguished without instrumental assistance. It turns out that the proposed method has good sensitivity, selectivity and potential application in sensing.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new kind of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely studied owing to possessing good biocompatibility, remarkable photostability, low toxicity, and easy to modification [1–7]. A variety of carbon dots such as nitrogen-doped, sulphur-doped, phosphorus doped, boron-doped, fluorine-doped and iron-doped CDs had been synthesized by diverse carbon precursors and different synthetic method [8–11]. These reported CDs inherently possessed unique chemical structures and various surface organic functional groups including oxygen-based functional groups such as carboxylic (–COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) groups, nitrogen-based functional groups such as pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and amino groups (–NH2) and sulphur-based groups [12–15]. Therefore, the CDs have more surface active sites to combine effectively with metal ion or indirectly coordinate with some nonmetallic ions. Based on the excellent properties, CDs can be designed as a promising sensor for the detection of various metal ions (such as, Hg2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Au3+, Pt2+, Pd2+, Co2+, Cr(VI)), nonmetallic ions (such as H+, F-, I-, CN–, ClO-, S2-, PO4 3-, C2O4 2-) and biomolecules (such as, amino acid, dopamine, ascorbic acid, glucose, adenine, DNA and protein) [9,16–22].

Chemiluminescence (CL) is a phenomenon of light radiation produced during the oxidation–reduction reaction [23]. The CL method is also a powerful analytical technique with high sensitivity, low noise ratio signal, fast responsiblility, and simple equipment [24,25]. These valuable advantages have promoted its wide application in clinical diagnostics, molecular biology environmental monitoring and food analysis fields [26–28]. Tan et al., [29] constructed a peroxyoxalate chemiluminescence (PO CL) recovery system based on the interaction of N-doped graphene oxide nanosheets and an oligopeptide for the ultra-sensitive detection of Cu2+ ions in environmental water samples. The detection limit of this method is as low as 0.2 pmol/L. Bailon et al., [30] developed a methamidophos sensor using the bis(2,4,6-trichlorophenyl) oxalate (TCPO)-rubrene-H2O2 CL system to detect the pesticide.

Thanks to excellent optical performance and stable chemical structure, CDs can be included into the CL reaction system. Such as, Cheng’s group [31] detected bromate by the redox reaction between CDs, bromate and sulfite in the acidic medium. It was suggested that the reaction mechanism of chemiluminiscence was the formation of hole-injected and electron-injected CDs through the redox reaction. Hassanzadeh et al., [32] design a chemiluminescent sensing strategy to be applied to the determination of Hg2+ in cosmetics, based on the quenching effect of Hg2+ on the produced emission of the CDs-TCPO-H2O2 CL system. However, although there are a few reports in the literature regarding application of CDs in CL systems, to the best of our knowledge, there has not been reported about bis(2-carbopentyloxy-3,5,6-trichlorophenyl) oxalate (CPPO)–H2O2-CDs CL system.

Herein, based on CPPO-H2O2-CDs CL reaction, this work constructed a visible luminous sensing platform (PO/CDs CL system), in which CDs acted as capturers and emitters for Fe3+ identification and luminescence signal. The novel visible luminous sensing platform showed enormous potential application in medicine and sensing.

Dibutyl phthalate (DBP) and bis(2-carbopentyloxy-3,5,6-trichlorophenyl) oxalate (CPPO) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Other chemicals used in this work were obtained from the Aladdin Reagent Co. ltd. (Shanghai, China). The red pitaya was purchased from local supermarket. Deionized water (18.2 MU) was used throughout for all the experiments.

The morphologies and size of the CDs were measured with transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100). The surface construction of the CDs was obtained through fourier infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Scientifific, Nicolet 6700) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientifific, Escalab 250xi). The degree of crystallinity of the CDs was measured with X-ray diffffraction (XRD, Shimadzu, XRD-700). The fluorescent property of the CDs was researched by UV–vis spectra (Shimadzu, UV-2550) and a spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu, RF-5301PC).

CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of red pitaya at 180 °C. In a typical synthesis, 5.0 g of red pitaya was mashed and mixed with 10 mL ultrapure water. Then hydrothermal reaction was performed in 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 10 h in an oven. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min to discard precipitation, then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove large particles. The obtained CDs solution was concentrated and freeze-dried for further use.

The QY of the CDs was determined according to the reported procedure [25]. In brief, quinine sulfate was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY: 0.54 at 340 nm). In order to minimize the re-absorption effects, the absorbencies in the 10 mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.05 at the excitation wavelength (360 nm). The QY was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
Q =
Q R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
n R 2
where symbolsQI, n, and A refer to the QY, the measured integrated emission intensity, the refractive index, and the absorbance of samples solutions, respectively. The subscript R stand for the corresponding parameter of known fluorescent standard substances.

The detection of Fe3+ was carried out in 10 mM phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.0). In a typical assay, 10 μL of CDs dispersion (30 mg⋅mL−1) was mixed with 2.99 mL of PBS solution and recorded the fluorescent data. Then different concentrations of Fe3+ were added. The solution reacted for 2 min at room temperature and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. To evaluate the selectivity of Fe3+ sensing, some common metal ions (including Hg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Na+, K+, Al3+ and Pb2+) were used instead of Fe3+ and recorded the fluorescence response follow the same procedure. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

Firstly, the chemiluminescent reaction solutions were prepared. The reaction solutions consisted of two components, named as solution I and solution II, respectively. The solution I was prepared by dissolving 10 g of CPPO in 50 mL DBP via water-bath heating treatments at 70 °C for 10 min. Solution II was prepared by dissolving 15 g sodium acetate into the mixture of 25 mL hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %) and 25 mL tert-butanol. When 1 mL of solution I, 1 mL of solution II and 1 mL of 1 mg⋅mL−1 CDs was mixed, bright yellow fluorescence can be observed. For Fe3+ assay, CDs was mixed in advance with different concentration of Fe3+ by following the same procedure described above. Emission spectra can be recorded by fluorescence spectrometer under turning off the exciting lights. For visible fluorescent assay, the images of system were taken using the smartphone camera.

The CDs were synthesized by simple hydrothermal treatment of red pitaya at 180 °C for 12 h. Fig. 1 A displayed a typical TEM image and size distribution histogram of the CDs, suggesting that the particle size lay within the limits of 4.7–7.5 nm, and the average diameter of particles was about 5.8 nm. And the high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image in the inset of Fig. 1A displayed that the lattice spacing was 0.21 nm attributed to the (100 facet) in-plane lattice fringe of graphene [33–36].

The XPS and FTIR spectrum were further performed to study the surface structure of these CDs. The XPS in Fig. 1B showed that the CDs were mainly composed of carbon (75.3 %) and oxygen (23.8 %) elements. In the high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1C), four peaks were observed at the binding energy peak of 284.5, 285.5, 287.5 and 288.6 eV, according to CC, CO, CO and C-OOR, respectively [33]. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 1D) displayed two strong peaks at 530.9 and 532.4 eV attributed to CO and C-OH/COC, respectively [37]. In Fig. 1E, the FTIR spectrum exhibited a broad band in the region of 3020–3500 cm−1 which was ascribed to stretching vibrations of OH. The bands at 1699 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 were corresponded to the stretching vibrations of CO and CO, respectively [38]. The peak at 1380 cm−1 can be assigned to –COOH groups. The bands around 1093 and 1236 cm−1 can be ascribed to the bending vibrations of CH [12,39–41]. The above results revealed that there existed graphite structures in the CDs and many O-related groups on their surface. XRD was also performed to characterize the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1F, the XRD pattern displayed a broad peak centred at 24.6°, which further confirmed the graphitic structure of the CDs [42,43].

Fig. 2 A showed the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CDs. A strong absorption peak can be observed at 278.8 nm, which was originated from π-π* transitions of sp2 -conjugation in the carbon core [41,44]. When irradiated with 365 nm in aqueous solution, the CDs emitted bright blue fluorescence (as shown in the inset of Fig. 2A). The maximum emission wavelength at 450 nm could be observed at excitation wavelength of 350 nm. And QY of the CDs was calculated to be ∼ 13 % with quinine sulfate as measurement standards, indicating the CDs enable to be used as fluorescent probes. The Fluorescence (FL) emission in Fig. 2B indicated that the CDs possessed excitation-dependent emission character. When the excitation wavelength increased from 310 to 440 nm, the emission peak red-shifted gradually and the strongest fluorescent spectrum was obtained at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm.

When a small amount of Fe3+ was added to the CDs solution, as shown in Fig. S1A, the solution has become cloudy. In addition, a comparison of the CDs solution and CDs/Fe3+ complex under 365 nm UV light showed that the colour of the CDs/Fe3+ complex became dark and the fluorescence of the CDs/Fe3+ complex was significantly reduced, as shown in Fig. S1B. TEM further confirmed the formation of the CDs/Fe3+ complex in the dispersion (Fig. S1C). What’s more, the EDS analysis of the CDs/Fe3+ complex revealed a large amount of C and O elements, as well as a small amount of Fe element, as shown in Figure S2. The UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. 3 A (curve b) showed that Fe3+ solution has a wide absorption bands from 260 to 390 nm. And the CDs (curve a) has a strong absorption peak centred at 282 nm in aqueous solution. However, in the presence of 50 μM Fe3+ (curve c), the absorption peaks of the CDs enhanced evidently, but the absorption band of Fe3+ ion disappeared, revealing the formation of CDs/Fe3+ complex. From the FL emission spectra in Fig. 3B, it can be observed that the CDs’ fluorescence was quenched to 35 % of original fluorescent intensity upon the addition of 50 μM Fe3+. The photograph in the inset of Fig. 3B further confirmed the transformation that the fluorescence can be quenched completely after adding excess Fe3+ (1 mM).

The mechanism of the optical response to Fe3+ can be explained from the surface groups of the CDs. The results of XPS and FTIR indicated that there exist abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the CDs surface, which are apt to react with Fe3+ to form multicomponent complexes. Based on it, we speculated that the fluorescence quenching of CDs was mostly ascribes to the complex reaction between O-related groups and Fe3+ ions [41,45,46]. The CDs/Fe3+ complex promoted electron transfer and restrained excition recombination, thus caused significant fluorescence quenching.

Fig. 3C and D showed the fluorescence detection of Fe3+ in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.0) under the optimized conditions. It turned out that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreased gradually upon the addition of Fe3+, and there was a good linear correlation between the quenching efficiency [(F0-F)/F0] and the concentration of Fe3+ in the range from 2.0 to 40 nM. The linear-regression equation is as following:
(1)
F 0
- F
/
F 0
= 0.00 328 c
nmol .
L
- 1
+ 0.00
76
(
R
2
= 0 . 996 )
where F0 and F indicate the fluorescent intensity of the CDs before and after quenching, and c is the concentration of Fe3+. Based on the linear equation, the limitation of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1.2 nM (S/N = 3), indicating the high sensitivity of the proposed method. It is well known that CDs have excellent photostability. The results in Fig. 3D also revealed that the standard deviation of response signal in different determination was lower than 3.8 %, suggesting predominant repeatability and precisions of the detection.

In addition, the selectivity of the assay was studied to evaluate the feasibility of application in practical samples. Some potentially interfering substances in environmental water (including Hg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Na+, K+, Al3+, Pb2+, SO3 2-, PO4 3-, CO3 2– and Cl-) were explored. The results in Fig. 4 showed that these common ions displayed little effect on the detection of Fe3+, indicating the feasibility of the method in practical application.

Afterwards, another sensing method was developed for the detection of Fe3+ based on the peroxyoxalate chemiluminescent reaction. Since the emission spectra of chemiluminescence are the same as that of fluorescence, signal outputs can be read by the fluorescence spectrometer. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the CDs can emit blue light with the emission wavelength at 446 nm under 365 nm irradiation (curve a and picture a), while cannot emit fluorescence without exciting light (curve b and picture b). Similarly, the mixture of solution I (CPPO dissolved in DBP) and solution II (containing H2O2, tert-butanol and sodium acetate) didn’t display significant luminescence (curve c and picture c in Fig. 5A). However, when the CDs were added into the above mixture solution, bright yellow chemiluminescence with the maximum emission wavelength at 535 nm was observed (curve d and picture d in Fig. 5A). Fig. 5B displayed the decay curves of the PO/CDs CL system. When the solution I, II and CDs were mixed thoroughly, the PO/CDs CL system exhibited strong yellow chemiluminescence, and the intensity declined gradually owing to the consume of raw material. The decay times can be calculated to be ∼ 36.5 s, thus the system can be used as a sensing platform by monitoring the light change in a certain time.

In addition, the application of the PO/CDs CL system in sensing was explored. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the PO/CDs CL system emitted bright yellow light, and its luminous intensity markedly decreased with the addition of Fe3+. When 5.0 mM of Fe3+ was added, the chemiluminescence was almost completely quenched. The above results confirmed the feasibility of applying the system in sensing. Fig. 6B-D displayed the effect of Fe3+ on the chemiluminescence of the PO/CDs CL system. The results indicated that the solution changed from yellow to dark brown with the increase of the concentration of Fe3+ simultaneously, and the chemiluminescence of the system was quenched gradually (as shown in Fig. 6B and D). Fig. 6C displayed good linearity of luminous intensity with the logarithm of the concentration of Fe3+. The linear-regression equation is as following:
(2)
F 0
- - F = 95 .
6 lg
c
nmol .
L
- 1
- -
38
.
2
(
R
2
= 0 . 9936 )

Based on the linear equation, the LOD was calculated to be 20 nM (S/N = 3), revealing the excellent sensitivity of the detection. The fluorescent images of the reaction in Fig. 6D indicated that the quenching of chemiluminescence can be clearly observed in the presence of 0.3 mM of Fe3+. What’s more, the results in Fig. 6C showed that the standard deviation of response signal in different determination was lower than 5.3 %, which indicated that the visible detection had good reliability and precision. However, the visible fluorescent method has apparent drawbacks. This method needs to be carried out in dark space and the ambient light intensity directly affects the sensitivity of the assay. In order to evaluate the performance of the designed sensing, we listed the optical determination of Fe3+ in Table S1. From the results, we can see that the mentioned method possessed good advantages in detection range and sensitivity, indicating the design have great prospects in visual determination.

The chemiluminescence mechanism of the CPPO-H2O2-CDs CL reaction was shown in Fig. 7 . The oxidation reaction of peroxyoxalate derivative (bis(2-carbopentyloxy-3,5,6-trichlorophenyl) oxalate by H2O2 produced a high-energy intermediate(HEI, 1,2-dioxetanedione), which possessed excess unstable energy and was transferred as a charge to fluorescent CDs that acted as an electron acceptor, producing a singlet excited state that emitted yellow chemiluminescence after de-excitation. And then, the PO/CDs CL system was suitable for the chemiluminescence quenching of Fe3+ and exhibited a high selectivity and sensitivity, owing to the chelation of Fe3+ with PO/CDs formed a stable non-radiative compound which was responsible for the quenching effect of CL emission. That is to mean that the chelation of Fe3+ with PO/CDs resulted in the transfer of non-radiative electrons from the excited state to the Fe3+ orbital, reducing the overall CL emission of the system.

The selectivity of the PO/CDs CL system was further explored using these common metal ions that may co-exist with Fe3+ in water environment. From Fig. 8 , it can be saw that the addition of 3 mM Fe3+ led to significant quenching, but the other metal ions only showed negligible effect on the assay, verifying the good selectivity in Fe3+ sensing.

A visible chemiluminescent assay platform was explored based on PO/CDs CL system, in which CDs acted as capturers and emitters. The PO/CDs CL system can emit strong yellow chemiluminescence with long decay times of ∼ 36.5 s. What’s more, the abundant O-related functional groups on CDs surface enable the system to specifically distinguish Fe3+ ion with low-concentration. By the PO/CDs CL system, the targets can be assayed in the dark, which efficiently avoids excessive dependence of large measuring instrument.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the Fundamental Research Funds for Central Universities (XDJK2019B001).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107782.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
